label: Oslo Accords
summary: The Oslo Accords were a landmark set of agreements signed in 1993 and 1995 between the Israeli government and the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO). The first accord, signed on the White House lawn in September 1993, established mutual recognition between Israel and the PLO and outlined a framework for Palestinian self-governance. Yasser Arafat signed on behalf of the PLO, and Yitzhak Rabin signed for Israel, with US President Bill Clinton presiding. The accords created the Palestinian Authority to govern parts of the West Bank and Gaza Strip and were intended as a stepping stone toward a final two-state settlement. Despite initial optimism, implementation stalled due to disagreements over Jerusalem, settlements, and Palestinian statehood, and the peace process effectively collapsed after 2000.
key_facts: Signed on September 13, 1993, at the White House by Yasser Arafat and Yitzhak Rabin
Created the Palestinian Authority to administer parts of the West Bank and Gaza
Recognized the PLO as the legitimate representative of the Palestinian people
Led to the Nobel Peace Prize for Arafat, Rabin, and Israeli Foreign Minister Shimon Peres in 1994
A second accord (Oslo II) was signed in 1995, further expanding Palestinian self-rule
sources: US Department of State — Middle East Peace Process
